answer:
In Johann Gottlieb Fichte's *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, the plurality of *Fürsten*—evoking the fragmented sovereignty of the Holy Roman Empire, where numerous German princes coexisted as semi-independent rulers—is central to the preface's opening gambit.[^1] Fichte observes that it is striking for critics to target "princes and the great" (*Fürsten und Grosse*) in an era when "most German princes" (*die mehresten der deutschen Fürsten*) actively seek popularity, dismantle outdated etiquette, and appear to value scholarship. This plurality undergirds his argument: he does not attack any *one* prince but seeks to "unite them all" (*sie alle zu verbinden*) through a shared call to protect freedom of thought, offering states a chance to demonstrate their enlightenment by allowing the pamphlet's publication. The German *Fürst* thus signals a collective of co-sovereigns whose decentralized power (unlike a unitary monarch) allows for reformist goodwill amid competition.

An English rendering as "prince" obscures this by connoting a singular heir apparent, minor lord, or fairy-tale figure—evoking personal absolutism rather than the HRE's fractured polity of hundreds of coexisting *Fürsten*. This flattens Fichte's strategic appeal to a plural, reformable class of rulers into a critique of isolated tyranny.[^2]

[^1]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, [paragraph](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/#p-0e25d1).

[^2]: The original German reinforces the plural: *die mehresten der deutschen Fürsten* ("most of the German princes"), underscoring multiplicity in a specifically German imperial context. Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, [paragraph](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/original/#p-d0367d).
